M01-title.title: Module 01
M01-title.subtitle: Opening & Cognitive Reframing
M01-title.notes: This is the most critical opening of the entire course. If the opening fails, everything that follows will be significantly less effective.

M01-objectives.title: Learning Objectives
M01-objectives.studentContent:
After completing this module, you will be able to:

  • Recognize the limitations of "gut-feeling evaluations"
  • Understand the value of a "Content Operating System"
  • Establish an overall impression of the "Bone-Meat-Soul Three-Layer Framework"
  • Develop intrinsic motivation to learn the BMS framework
M01-objectives.presenterContent: 💡 Teaching Tip: These 4 objectives are the core deliverables of this module. All subsequent content is designed around these 4 objectives.
M01-objectives.notes: Quickly go through the learning objectives; no need to elaborate.

M01-part1-title.title: Part 1
M01-part1-title.subtitle: Opening Warm-Up
M01-part1-title.notes: The key to the opening is building a sense of anticipation.

M01-1-opening.title: Welcome to the BMS Bootcamp
M01-1-opening.studentContent:
"This video is good"
What does that actually mean?
Today is not about teaching you how to shoot videos
It's about teaching you an "Operating System" for evaluating and creating video content
M01-1-opening.presenterContent:
📢 Presenter Script
Hello everyone, welcome to the BMS Bootcamp.

Every day we watch videos, evaluate videos, and create videos... but what does "this video is good" actually mean?

Today is not about teaching you how to shoot videos. It's about teaching you an "Operating System" for evaluating and creating video content.

After this training, you'll be able to discuss content using a shared language, and even have AI help you write scripts.
M01-1-opening.notes: The opening 3 sentences should be concise and impactful. Speak at a moderate pace to give participants time to absorb.

M01-1-icebreaker.title: Quick Introductions
M01-1-icebreaker.studentContent:
🤝 Interactive Session
15-Second Self-Introduction:
Your name + the product line you're responsible for

Before we begin, I'd like to ask everyone a question:

How do you usually judge whether a video is good or not?
M01-1-icebreaker.presenterContent:
💡 Presenter Tip:
  • If there are more than 15 people, have them introduce by group or select a few
  • This segment should not exceed 5 minutes
  • Collect 2-3 answers to the icebreaker question, just say "Great, thanks" without commenting
M01-1-icebreaker.notes: The icebreaker question sets the stage for the Blank Paper Experiment. Participants' vague answers will confirm the conclusions later.

M01-part2-title.title: Part 2
M01-part2-title.subtitle: Blank Paper Experiment
M01-part2-title.notes: The Blank Paper Experiment is the centerpiece of this module! Do not skip any part of it.

M01-2-rules.title: Experiment Rules
M01-2-rules.studentContent:
Next, I will play a video

Please write down your answer on paper:

"Why is this video good? Or why is it not good?"
⚠️ Please think independently. Do not discuss with the person next to you.
M01-2-rules.presenterContent: ▶ Presenter Action: Make sure every participant has blank paper and a pen
M01-2-rules.notes: Emphasizing "think independently" is important so that different perspectives become visible later.

M01-2-video.title: Watch the Video
M01-2-video.studentContent:
🎬
Select a Video to Play
▶ Video A▶ Video B
Opens video analysis tool in a new tab
M01-2-video.presenterContent:
💡 Video Selection Tips:
Choose a controversial video (not an obvious hit or obvious failure) to spark different opinions.
Using your company's own product videos is recommended, as participants will relate more.
M01-2-video.notes: Video selection is critical! Choose a video that will elicit different evaluations. Clicking the button opens the BMS Training Assistant player in a new tab.

M01-2-writing.title: Independent Writing
M01-2-writing.studentContent:
✍️
Write a few keywords, or write a paragraph — it's up to you
M01-2-writing.presenterContent:
▶ Presenter Action:
  • Walk slowly around the room, observing participants' writing progress
  • Do not read their specific content — maintain the element of surprise
  • If anyone is discussing, gently remind them: "Please think independently"
  • Give a 1-minute warning: "One minute left"
M01-2-writing.notes: 8 minutes is carefully designed — enough time for serious thought, but not too long. The presenter should walk around but not intervene.

M01-2-sharing.title: Sharing Session
M01-2-sharing.studentContent:
🤝 Interactive Session
Let's have a few participants share their answers
Feeling-Based
"It feels high-quality" "It looks premium"
Element-Based
"The music is great" "The visuals are beautiful"
Technical
"The pacing is fast" "Lots of transitions"
Vague
"I can't really explain why"
M01-2-sharing.presenterContent:
💡 Selection Strategy:
  • Select 5-8 people (adjust based on class size)
  • Deliberately choose people who appear to have different answers
  • Don't select everyone from the same area
M01-2-sharing.notes: This is the session for collecting "evidence." The different answers are precisely the proof needed to highlight the problem later.

M01-2-problem.title: Discovering the Problem
M01-2-problem.studentContent:
Did you notice a problem?
We don't have a shared language
M01-2-problem.presenterContent:
📢 Presenter Script (Key Moment!)
(Looking at everyone, pause for 3 seconds)

Did you notice a problem?

(Pause for 5 seconds, let everyone think)

We don't have a shared language.
⚠️ Tone Guidance: This sentence should be delivered calmly and slowly — not as a reproach, but as a statement of fact. Let participants feel the severity of the problem themselves.
M01-2-problem.notes: This is the climax of the entire Blank Paper Experiment! "We don't have a shared language" needs sufficient pause and weight.

M01-2-consequence.title: What Does This Mean?
M01-2-consequence.studentContent:
All of your answers made sense. But we were evaluating from completely different angles:

  • Some talked about feelings, others about visuals
  • Some mentioned data, others couldn't explain why...
If a team doesn't have a unified evaluation framework, it cannot replicate success
"Your viral hit was luck, not skill."
M01-2-consequence.notes: Core argument: No shared language = Cannot replicate success = Viral hits depend on luck.

M01-2-classify.title: Three-Column Classification Exercise
M01-2-classify.studentContent:
🤝 Interactive Session
Please complete the following task:
  1. Look at the evaluation words you wrote on your paper
  2. Try to sort them into three categories and fill them into three columns
  3. Give each column a heading
There is no right answer. Categorize them however you see fit.
⏱ Time: 5 minutes
M01-2-classify.presenterContent:
▶ Presenter Action: Distribute three-column sheets, prepare scissors and glue sticks (or have participants write their categories directly)
💡 Common Participant Categorizations:
  • "Visual / Audio / Feeling"
  • "Content / Form / Effect"
  • "Good / Average / Uncertain"
M01-2-classify.notes: This exercise lets participants actively discover the need for a unified framework, rather than passively accepting it.

M01-2-discovery.title: Show & Discover
M01-2-discovery.studentContent:
Everyone categorized differently and used different headings
What does that tell us?
If everyone on the team categorizes things differently,
we still don't have a shared language...
M01-2-discovery.presenterContent:
📢 Presenter Script
Would a few participants please hold up your three-column sheets for everyone to see?

(Looking at the displayed categorizations) Interesting...

Did everyone notice? Each person categorized differently and used different headings.
What does that tell us?
🎯 Key Moment: Guide participants to say it themselves: "We need a unified classification standard"
M01-2-discovery.notes: This is the "aha" moment for participants. Guide them to discover it on their own.

M01-2-mission.title: Today's Mission
M01-2-mission.studentContent:
What we're going to do today is:

1️⃣ Unify the headings of these three columns
2️⃣ Break down the contents of each column in detail
This is the system we'll be learning today —
Bone · Meat · Soul
M01-2-mission.notes: The BMS framework officially debuts! A natural transition from the participants' classification exercise.

M01-2-before-after.title: BEFORE vs AFTER
M01-2-before-after.studentContent:
❌ BEFORE: Blank-Paper Style Evaluation
  • "This video has great texture"
  • "The music is nice"
  • "It feels premium"
✗ Not replicable ✗ Not transferable ✗ Not optimizable
✅ AFTER: Structured Analysis
  • 【Bone】HOOK uses SUSPENSE_QUESTION, Strategy HAND, Structure HPVC
  • 【Soul】Target audience: new moms, Emotion: anxiety → identification → hope
  • 【Meat】Pacing: high, Talking-head format, High editing density
✓ Replicable ✓ Transferable ✓ Optimizable
M01-2-before-after.presenterContent:
📢 Presenter Script
(Show BEFORE) This is where we started today.

(Show AFTER) This is where we'll be in two days.

When you can evaluate a video using language like this,
you're no longer "someone who shoots videos" —
You're a "Content System Architect."
M01-2-before-after.notes: This is the most visually impactful comparison! Don't explain the AFTER details yet — leave them as a teaser.

M01-part3-title.title: Part 3
M01-part3-title.subtitle: Cognitive Reframing
M01-part3-title.notes: The cognitive reframing section can be covered at a slightly faster pace; detailed content will be expanded in later modules.

M01-3-why-os.title: Why Do We Need an "Operating System"
M01-3-why-os.studentContent:
Dimension Gut-Feeling Evaluation Operating System
Vocabulary "Catchy" "Has a vibe" "Feels right" "HOOK-PROBLEM-VALUE-CTA"
Replicability Low (depends on individual experience) High (teachable, learnable, iterable)
AI Collaboration Difficult (AI doesn't understand feelings) Efficient (structured input = structured output)
Team Communication "You know what I mean?" "This HOOK uses L2-Question"
M01-3-why-os.presenterContent: 💡 Key Emphasis: Focus on the "AI Collaboration" row — this is the practical value participants care about most.
M01-3-why-os.notes: Use the comparison table to clearly show participants the difference between the two approaches.

M01-3-overview.title: The Bone-Meat-Soul Three-Layer Framework
M01-3-overview.studentContent:
Bone

Bone

How is it structured?

Skeleton Type
Strategy & Persuasion Techniques

Soul

Soul

Why does it resonate?

Target Audience, Trigger Scenario
Reward Type, Conflict Type, Emotional Arc

Meat

Meat

How is it presented?

Visuals, Audio, Pacing
Subtitles, Effects, Duration
M01-3-overview.presenterContent:
📢 Presenter Script
Bone answers the question "How is it structured?" What is the content's skeleton? What strategy is used to persuade?

Soul answers "Why does it resonate?" What emotion does it trigger? What reward does it offer? What psychological tension does it resolve?

Meat answers "What does it look and sound like?" What visuals, pacing, and audio are used?
M01-3-overview.notes: The first official reveal of the BMS framework. Help participants form an overall impression.

M01-3-order.title: Why the Order Is Bone → Soul → Meat
M01-3-order.studentContent:
• 1. Bone First: Without structure, emotion and visuals are unfocused
  • 2. Soul Second: Only after understanding the structure can you grasp the emotional design of each section
  • 3. Meat Last: Visuals serve the structure and emotion
⚠️ Common Misconception: Many people want to learn "how to make it look good" first, but "looking good" is only meaningful when there's a clear structural objective.
M01-3-order.notes: Explain the logic behind the learning sequence. Correct the misconception of "learn filming techniques first."

M01-3-closing.title: The Cognitive Shift
M01-3-closing.studentContent:
The next time you see a video, don't ask:
"Is this video good or bad?"
Instead, ask:
"What is this video's Bone? Its Soul? Its Meat?"
M01-3-closing.presenterContent: 🎯 Cognitive Shift Check: Participants should shift from "Is it good or bad?" → "What is the Bone / Soul / Meat?"
M01-3-closing.notes: Module wrap-up. Make sure participants have completed the cognitive shift before continuing.

M01-end.title: Module 01 Complete ✓
M01-end.subtitle: Up Next: Module 02 Bone System
M01-end.notes: M01 complete. After a short break, proceed to M02 Bone System — the most important module of the entire training.
